liouville.title: Liouville's theorem
liouville.statement: A bounded entire function is constant.

terms.label: $f'(z_0)$ — the derivative at an arbitrary point

steps.title: the hypotheses
steps.note: Two assumptions, and the second is global: one fixed $M$ works at every point of the plane at once. That single number is the lever the whole proof pulls.

steps.title: Cauchy's estimate with n = 1
steps.note: Pick any point $z_0$ and any circle of radius $R$ around it. Entire means holomorphic on every closed disk, so the estimate from earlier in this lesson applies at every radius — the hypothesis CIF needs comes free.

steps.title: let R → ∞
steps.note: Boundedness earns its keep: $M$ is pinned while $R$ is ours, so the bound can be driven as small as we like. For an unbounded function, $M$ would grow along with $R$ and this lever wouldn't exist.

steps.title: the squeeze
steps.note: $f'(z_0)$ is a single fixed number sitting below an arbitrarily small bound. The only nonnegative number smaller than every $M/R$ is $0$.

steps.title: z₀ was arbitrary
steps.note: Nothing above chose $z_0$ specially, so the derivative vanishes at every point. $f$ is constant — Liouville's theorem.

fta.title: The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
fta.statement: Every nonconstant polynomial over $\mathbb{C}$ has at least one root.

terms.label: $n \ge 1$ — the degree, waiting to bite back

steps.title: the setup, and the assumption to kill
steps.math: \htmlClass{pf-p}{p}(z) = a_{\htmlClass{pf-n}{n}} z^{\htmlClass{pf-n}{n}} + \dots + a_0, \quad \htmlClass{pf-n}{n} \ge 1, \ a_{\htmlClass{pf-n}{n}} \ne 0; \qquad \text{suppose } \htmlClass{pf-p}{p}(z) \ne 0 \ \text{ for every } z \in \mathbb{C}
steps.note: Degree $n \ge 1$ with a nonzero leading coefficient is exactly what "nonconstant" means — file that away, it returns at the very end. Everything after this line is a consequence of the assumption no root anywhere, held only so it can destroy itself.

steps.title: the assumption builds a function
steps.note: Pure setup, no cleverness: $p$ is holomorphic everywhere and — by assumption — never zero, so the quotient $f$ is defined and holomorphic on all of $\mathbb{C}$.

steps.title: far away: the leading term takes over
steps.note: For huge $|z|$ the term $a_nz^n$ dwarfs every other term of $p$ — this is the one place the polynomial's shape gets used. So outside some large disk, $f$ is not just bounded but tiny.

steps.title: close in: continuity does the rest
steps.math: \htmlClass{pf-f}{f} \ \text{ continuous on the closed disk} \implies |\htmlClass{pf-f}{f}| \le M \ \text{ there}
steps.note: Still setup. There is no singularity hiding in the disk — $p$ never vanishes, by assumption — and a continuous function on a closed disk is bounded. (This is the step that feels skippable and isn't: $|f| \to 0$ far away says nothing about the middle.)

steps.title: combine the two regions
steps.math: \htmlClass{pf-f}{f} \ \text{ is entire and bounded on all of } \mathbb{C}
steps.note: Small outside the disk, at most $M$ inside it: one bound covers the whole plane. Notice what just happened — the assumption "$p$ has no root" has manufactured exactly the hypotheses of Liouville's theorem.

steps.title: Liouville fires
steps.note: Everything before this was scaffolding; this is the only move that closes a loop. Liouville (previous section) forces the bounded entire $f$ to be constant, so $p$ is constant — but step 1 declared $n \ge 1$. The assumption was false: $p$ must have a root.
